statement: Foreign direct investment into the UK declined markedly following the 2016 Brexit vote. FDI inflows fell from an average of £50 billion per year (2013–2016) to approximately £30 billion per year (2017–2024). The UK's share of total European FDI fell from approximately 25% to 15% over the same period, with Ireland, the Netherlands, and Germany absorbing much of the redirected investment. Financial services were particularly affected, with an estimated 7,000 jobs relocating from London to EU financial centres (Dublin, Frankfurt, Paris, Amsterdam) by 2024. However, the UK attracted significant investment in technology, life sciences, and renewable energy, suggesting that FDI effects were sector-specific rather than universal.
source_name: OECD — FDI statistics; EY European Attractiveness Survey; City of London Corporation reports
caveats: FDI figures are volatile and affected by large individual transactions. Global FDI trends (declining since 2016) affect the comparison. The UK's FDI decline may partly reflect maturation of the economy rather than Brexit alone. Iceland's FDI profile (dominated by energy-intensive industry) is very different from the UK's.

related_entries.statement: UK goods trade with the EU declined significantly following Brexit. UK exports to the EU fell by approximately 15% in real terms between 2019 and 2024, while imports from the EU fell by approximately 10%. The UK's goods trade deficit with the EU widened from £94 billion (2019) to approximately £120 billion (2024). Non-tariff barriers — customs declarations, rules of origin documentation, sanitary and phytosanitary checks — proved more costly than tariffs for many businesses. The UK government's own analysis estimated that Brexit reduced long-run UK GDP by approximately 4% compared to continued EU membership. Services trade was less affected, as the UK maintained some market access through the Trade and Cooperation Agreement (TCA).

Foreign direct investment flows to Iceland have been highly volatile. FDI surged to $6.9 billion in 2007 (peak pre-crisis, driven by aluminium smelter investment), collapsed to $64 million in 2009, and turned sharply negative in 2016–2020 (net outflows totalling -$10.1 billion, as Icelandic firms expanded abroad while foreign investment slowed). FDI recovered in 2021–2024, reaching $2.7 billion in 2024. EU membership proponents argue that joining the single market would attract more stable FDI by reducing regulatory uncertainty and currency risk, citing Ireland's experience of massive FDI inflows post-EU accession.
